properties.name: mindtouch.page#overview
properties.value: This text takes up the fundamental concepts of general, organic, and biological chemistry. Emphasis is placed on the relationship between chemistry and the health/medical sciences, so the course is especially appropriate for students who intend to pursue a career in nursing and other health-related professions, including kinesiology and psychology.

summary: This text takes up the fundamental concepts of general, organic, and biological chemistry. Emphasis is placed on the relationship between chemistry and the health/medical sciences, so the course is especially appropriate for students who intend to pursue a career in nursing and other health-related professions, including kinesiology and psychology.
tags: lulu@Chemistry 110: An Introduction to General, Organic and Biological Chemistry@Garima Garg@Rio Hondo College@Chemistry 110: An Introduction to General, Organic and Biological Chemistry
title: Chemistry 110: An Introduction to General, Organic and Biological Chemistry (Garg)

properties.name: mindtouch.page#overview
properties.value: Chemistry is the branch of science dealing with the structure, composition, properties, and the reactive characteristics of matter. Matter is anything that has mass and occupies space. Thus, chemistry is the study of literally everything around us—the liquids that we drink, the gases we breathe, the composition of everything from the plastic case on your phone to the earth beneath your feet. Moreover, chemistry is the study of the transformation of matter.

summary: Chemistry is the branch of science dealing with the structure, composition, properties, and the reactive characteristics of matter. Matter is anything that has mass and occupies space. Thus, chemistry is the study of literally everything around us—the liquids that we drink, the gases we breathe, the composition of everything from the plastic case on your phone to the earth beneath your feet. Moreover, chemistry is the study of the transformation of matter.
title: 1.1: Chemicals Compose Ordinary Things

properties.name: mindtouch.page#overview
properties.value: Science is a process of knowing about the natural universe through observation and experiment. Scientists go through a rigorous process to determine new knowledge about the universe; this process is generally referred to as the scientific method. Science is broken down into various fields, including chemistry, and is both qualitative and quantitative.

summary: Science is a process of knowing about the natural universe through observation and experiment. Scientists go through a rigorous process to determine new knowledge about the universe; this process is generally referred to as the scientific method. Science is broken down into various fields, including chemistry, and is both qualitative and quantitative.
title: 1.3: The Scientific Method - How Chemists Think

properties.name: mindtouch.page#overview
properties.value: Most people can succeed in chemistry, but it often requires dedication, hard work, the right attitude and study habits!

summary: Most people can succeed in chemistry, but it often requires dedication, hard work, the right attitude and study habits!
title: 1.4: A Beginning Chemist - How to Succeed

properties.name: mindtouch.page#overview
properties.value: Measurements representing properties quantitatively, Units, i.e., quantities defined by the standard that peoples agree to use as a reference, systems of units, i.e., a set of units used for measurements, and basic mathematics involved in handling the units are described.

summary: Measurements representing properties quantitatively, Units, i.e., quantities defined by the standard that peoples agree to use as a reference, systems of units, i.e., a set of units used for measurements, and basic mathematics involved in handling the units are described.
title: 1.5: Measurements

properties.name: mindtouch.page#overview
properties.value: Chemists often work with numbers that are exceedingly large or small. For example, entering the mass in grams of a hydrogen atom into a calculator requires a display with at least 24 decimal places. A system called scientific notation avoids much of the tedium and awkwardness of manipulating numbers with large or small magnitudes.

summary: Chemists often work with numbers that are exceedingly large or small. For example, entering the mass in grams of a hydrogen atom into a calculator requires a display with at least 24 decimal places. A system called scientific notation avoids much of the tedium and awkwardness of manipulating numbers with large or small magnitudes.
title: 1.6: Scientific Notation - Writing Large and Small Numbers

properties.name: mindtouch.page#overview
properties.value: Chemists measure the properties of matter and express these measurements as quantities. A quantity is an amount of something and consists of a number and a unit. The number tells us how many (or how much), and the unit tells us what the scale of measurement is. For example, when a distance is reported as “5 kilometers,” we know that the quantity has been expressed in units of kilometers and that the number of kilometers is 5.

summary: Chemists measure the properties of matter and express these measurements as quantities. A quantity is an amount of something and consists of a number and a unit. The number tells us how many (or how much), and the unit tells us what the scale of measurement is. For example, when a distance is reported as “5 kilometers,” we know that the quantity has been expressed in units of kilometers and that the number of kilometers is 5.
title: 2.1: Taking Measurements

properties.name: mindtouch.page#overview
properties.value: Metric prefixes derive from Latin or Greek terms. The prefixes are used to make the units manageable. The SI system is based on multiples of ten. There are seven basic units in the SI system. Five of these units are commonly used in chemistry.

summary: Metric prefixes derive from Latin or Greek terms. The prefixes are used to make the units manageable. The SI system is based on multiples of ten. There are seven basic units in the SI system. Five of these units are commonly used in chemistry.
title: 2.2: The Basic Units of Measurement

properties.name: mindtouch.page#overview
properties.value: The SI base units specifies certain units for various types of quantities, based on seven fundamental units. We will use most of the fundamental units in chemistry. This section addresses the measurements: mass, length, and volume

summary: The SI base units specifies certain units for various types of quantities, based on seven fundamental units. We will use most of the fundamental units in chemistry. This section addresses the measurements: mass, length, and volume
title: 2.3: Measuring Mass, Length, and Volume

properties.name: mindtouch.page#overview
properties.value: Significant figures properly report the number of measured and estimated digits in a measurement. There are rules for applying significant figures in calculations.

summary: Significant figures properly report the number of measured and estimated digits in a measurement. There are rules for applying significant figures in calculations.
title: 2.4: Measurement and Significant Figures

properties.name: mindtouch.page#overview
properties.value: To round a number, first decide how many significant figures the number should have. Once you know that, round to that many digits, starting from the left. If the number immediately to the right of the last significant digit is less than 5, it is dropped and the value of the last significant digit remains the same. If the number immediately to the right of the last significant digit is greater than or equal to 5, the last significant digit is increased by 1.

summary: To round a number, first decide how many significant figures the number should have. Once you know that, round to that many digits, starting from the left. If the number immediately to the right of the last significant digit is less than 5, it is dropped and the value of the last significant digit remains the same. If the number immediately to the right of the last significant digit is greater than or equal to 5, the last significant digit is increased by 1.
title: 2.5: Significant Figures in Calculations

properties.name: mindtouch.page#overview
properties.value: During your studies of chemistry (and physics also), you will note that mathematical equations are used in a number of different applications. Many of these equations have a number of different variables with which you will need to work. Note also that these equations will often require the use of measurements with their units. Algebra skills become very important here!

summary: During your studies of chemistry (and physics also), you will note that mathematical equations are used in a number of different applications. Many of these equations have a number of different variables with which you will need to work. Note also that these equations will often require the use of measurements with their units. Algebra skills become very important here!
title: 2.6: Problem Solving and Unit Conversions

properties.name: mindtouch.page#overview
properties.value: Sometimes you will have to perform more than one conversion to obtain the desired unit.

summary: Sometimes you will have to perform more than one conversion to obtain the desired unit.
title: 2.7: Solving Multi-step Conversion Problems

properties.name: mindtouch.page#overview
properties.value: Density is a physical property found by dividing the mass of an object by its volume. Regardless of the sample size, density is always constant.

summary: Density is a physical property found by dividing the mass of an object by its volume. Regardless of the sample size, density is always constant.
title: 2.8: Density

properties.name: mindtouch.page#overview
properties.value: Conversion factors are important in calculating dosages.

summary: Conversion factors are important in calculating dosages.
title: 2.9: Dosage Calculations

properties.name: mindtouch.page#overview
properties.value: Matter is anything that has mass and volume (takes up space). For most common objects that we deal with every day, it is fairly simple to demonstrate that they have mass and take up space. You might be able to imagine, however, the difficulty for people several hundred years ago to demonstrate that air has mass and volume. Air (and all other gases) are invisible to the eye, have very small masses compared to equal amounts of solids and liquids, and are quite easy to compress (change volume).

summary: Matter is anything that has mass and volume (takes up space). For most common objects that we deal with every day, it is fairly simple to demonstrate that they have mass and take up space. You might be able to imagine, however, the difficulty for people several hundred years ago to demonstrate that air has mass and volume. Air (and all other gases) are invisible to the eye, have very small masses compared to equal amounts of solids and liquids, and are quite easy to compress (change volume).
title: 3.1: What is Matter?

properties.name: mindtouch.page#overview
properties.value: One useful way of organizing our understanding of matter is to think of a hierarchy that extends down from the most general and complex, to the simplest and most fundamental. Matter can be classified into two broad categories: pure substances and mixtures. A pure substance is a form of matter that has a consistent composition and properties that are constant throughout the sample. A material composed of two or more substances is a mixture.

summary: One useful way of organizing our understanding of matter is to think of a hierarchy that extends down from the most general and complex, to the simplest and most fundamental. Matter can be classified into two broad categories: pure substances and mixtures. A pure substance is a form of matter that has a consistent composition and properties that are constant throughout the sample. A material composed of two or more substances is a mixture.
title: 3.2: Classifying Matter According to Its Composition

properties.name: mindtouch.page#overview
properties.value: Three states of matter exist—solid, liquid, and gas. Solids have a definite shape and volume. Liquids have a definite volume, but take the shape of the container. Gases have no definite shape or volume.

summary: Three states of matter exist—solid, liquid, and gas. Solids have a definite shape and volume. Liquids have a definite volume, but take the shape of the container. Gases have no definite shape or volume.
title: 3.3: Classifying Matter According to Its State—Solid, Liquid, and Gas

properties.name: mindtouch.page#overview
properties.value: A physical property is a characteristic of a substance that can be observed or measured without changing the identity of the substance. Physical properties include color, density, hardness, melting points, and boiling points. A chemical property describes the ability of a substance to undergo a specific chemical change.

summary: A physical property is a characteristic of a substance that can be observed or measured without changing the identity of the substance. Physical properties include color, density, hardness, melting points, and boiling points. A chemical property describes the ability of a substance to undergo a specific chemical change.
title: 3.4: Differences in Matter- Physical and Chemical Properties

properties.name: mindtouch.page#overview
properties.value: Change is happening all around us all of the time. Just as chemists have classified elements and compounds, they have also classified types of changes. Changes are either classified as physical or chemical changes. Chemists learn a lot about the nature of matter by studying the changes that matter can undergo. Chemists make a distinction between two different types of changes that they study—physical changes and chemical changes.

summary: Change is happening all around us all of the time. Just as chemists have classified elements and compounds, they have also classified types of changes. Changes are either classified as physical or chemical changes. Chemists learn a lot about the nature of matter by studying the changes that matter can undergo. Chemists make a distinction between two different types of changes that they study—physical changes and chemical changes.
title: 3.5: Changes in Matter - Physical and Chemical Changes
